title: Why TypeScript exists
instruction:
JavaScript is great until a typo causes a production bug at 2am. TypeScript catches mistakes before your code runs.

Run this and see typed functions in action.
successMessage: TypeScript guaranteed `salary` and `rate` are numbers. If you passed a string, the compiler would refuse — before the code ever runs.

title: Type annotation syntax — : type
instruction:
Add `: type` after a variable name or parameter to annotate its type. TypeScript checks every use against the annotation.

Annotate variables of different types.
successMessage: Four primitive types: `string`, `number`, `boolean`, and another `string`. TypeScript enforces the types — assigning `version = "five"` would be a compile error.

title: Type inference — TypeScript reads your code
instruction:
TypeScript infers types from initial values. You don't need to annotate every variable — the compiler figures it out.

See inference in action.
successMessage: TypeScript inferred all three types. Once inferred, the type is locked — you can't reassign a different type. Inference means less to write without sacrificing safety.

title: Function return types
instruction:
Annotating a function's return type creates a contract: TypeScript verifies every `return` statement matches the declared type.

Write a function with an explicit return type.
successMessage: `: string` and `: boolean` are the return type annotations. TypeScript would error if `formatPrice` returned a number or `isEven` returned a string. The return type documents intent AND enforces it.

title: TypeScript vs JavaScript — same runtime
instruction:
TypeScript compiles to plain JavaScript. All the type annotations disappear — there's zero runtime overhead. The types only exist during development.

See how TypeScript code produces clean output.
expectedOutput: Types exist only at compile time
successMessage: TypeScript is a compile-time tool only. The compiled JavaScript has no type annotations. Zero performance overhead — your types help the developer, not the browser.

title: Strict mode — maximum safety
instruction:
TypeScript's `strict: true` option enables the strongest type checking. It catches more bugs at the cost of being more explicit.

See what strict mode enforces.
expectedOutput: Hello Alice, you are a admin
Hello Bob, you are a viewer
successMessage: Strict mode (`"strict": true` in tsconfig.json) requires explicit types on parameters and treats `null`/`undefined` as distinct types. Every serious TypeScript project uses it.

title: Arrays and type inference
instruction:
TypeScript infers array types from their elements. Operations on typed arrays are checked — you can't push a wrong type or index incorrectly.

Work with typed arrays.
successMessage: TypeScript infers `string[]` for the languages array. `.push(42)` would be rejected — numbers aren't strings. The `mixed` array infers a union type `(string | number | boolean)[]`.

title: Objects — typing the shape
instruction:
TypeScript checks object shapes. If you define a variable as having `{ name: string; age: number }`, TypeScript enforces both properties exist and have the right types.

Create and use a typed object.
successMessage: The inline object type `{ name: string; age: number; email: string }` is checked at every use. Accessing non-existent properties or assigning wrong types is caught immediately.

title: Union types — either/or
instruction:
A union type `A | B` means a value can be either type A or type B. You must handle both possibilities.

Write a function that handles a string or number.
successMessage: Inside `if (typeof id === "string")`, TypeScript narrows the type to `string` — `.toUpperCase()` is available. In the `else` branch, it's `number` — `.toFixed()` is available. This is type narrowing.

title: Optional properties — ? in interfaces
instruction:
Mark a property with `?` to make it optional. TypeScript tracks that it might be `undefined` and requires you to handle that case.

Create an interface with optional fields.
expectedOutput: [2024-01-15] TypeScript Tips by Alice — tags: ts, programming
[draft] SQL Guide by Bob — tags: none
successMessage: `?` makes a property optional — the object can omit it. TypeScript knows it might be `undefined`, so `post.tags?.join()` uses optional chaining. `??` provides the fallback for `undefined`.

title: Type aliases — name your types
instruction:
A `type` alias gives a name to any type — primitives, objects, unions, arrays. Use them to make complex types readable and reusable.

Create type aliases for a payment system.
successMessage: `type` aliases make types readable. `Currency` restricts to three currencies — `"JPY"` would be a type error. `Status` limits the valid states. The `Payment` type bundles them all together.

title: The compiler catches real bugs
instruction:
The best way to appreciate TypeScript is to see it catch an actual bug. Here's a common JavaScript mistake that TypeScript prevents at compile time.

See TypeScript prevent a bug silently introduced by a refactor.
expectedOutput: Welcome, Alice Chen! Your account: alice@co.com
Welcome, Bob Taylor! Your account: bob@co.com
successMessage: In a JavaScript codebase, renaming `fullName` to `displayName` would require a manual search across every file — and any missed call site would silently send `undefined` in emails. TypeScript finds every usage at compile time.
